verses.text: Ahaz of Judah does evil like Israel's kings, even burning his own son in fire and sacrificing on every high hill. He plunges to the depths of pagan abomination. A heart that abandons God will, in time, embrace horrors it once would have abhorred.

Ahaz of Judah does evil like Israel's kings, even burning his own son in fire and sacrificing on every high hill. He plunges to the depths of pagan abomination. A heart that abandons God will, in time, embrace horrors it once would have abhorred.

Ahaz of Judah does evil like Israel's kings, even burning his own son in fire and sacrificing on every high hill. He plunges to the depths of pagan abomination. A heart that abandons God will, in time, embrace horrors it once would have abhorred.

Ahaz of Judah does evil like Israel's kings, even burning his own son in fire and sacrificing on every high hill. He plunges to the depths of pagan abomination. A heart that abandons God will, in time, embrace horrors it once would have abhorred.

Syria and Israel come up to besiege Jerusalem but cannot overcome it. The threat is real and frightening. Yet, as Isaiah urged him, Ahaz had only to trust God — the very thing his faithless heart would not do.

Syria and Israel come up to besiege Jerusalem but cannot overcome it. The threat is real and frightening. Yet, as Isaiah urged him, Ahaz had only to trust God — the very thing his faithless heart would not do.

Instead, Ahaz sends temple and palace silver to Assyria, begging, 'I am thy servant,' and Assyria duly crushes Syria. He buys deliverance by selling himself to a greater predator. Trusting the world's power against our fears only makes us its slaves.

Instead, Ahaz sends temple and palace silver to Assyria, begging, 'I am thy servant,' and Assyria duly crushes Syria. He buys deliverance by selling himself to a greater predator. Trusting the world's power against our fears only makes us its slaves.

Instead, Ahaz sends temple and palace silver to Assyria, begging, 'I am thy servant,' and Assyria duly crushes Syria. He buys deliverance by selling himself to a greater predator. Trusting the world's power against our fears only makes us its slaves.

Visiting Assyria's king, Ahaz admires a pagan altar at Damascus, sends its pattern home, and has one built to offer on. He imports idolatry as fashion. The faithless heart copies the world's worship, eager to be like the powers it fears.

Visiting Assyria's king, Ahaz admires a pagan altar at Damascus, sends its pattern home, and has one built to offer on. He imports idolatry as fashion. The faithless heart copies the world's worship, eager to be like the powers it fears.

He moves the LORD's bronze altar aside to make room for his new one, and the priest Urijah obeys without protest. The true worship is shoved to the margin. When leaders compromise, too often the clergy comply rather than resist.

He moves the LORD's bronze altar aside to make room for his new one, and the priest Urijah obeys without protest. The true worship is shoved to the margin. When leaders compromise, too often the clergy comply rather than resist.

He moves the LORD's bronze altar aside to make room for his new one, and the priest Urijah obeys without protest. The true worship is shoved to the margin. When leaders compromise, too often the clergy comply rather than resist.

Ahaz dismantles the temple furnishings and alters its structure 'for the king of Assyria.' He vandalizes God's house to flatter a foreign master. Fear of man will strip the sacred bare to win the favour of those it dreads.

Ahaz dismantles the temple furnishings and alters its structure 'for the king of Assyria.' He vandalizes God's house to flatter a foreign master. Fear of man will strip the sacred bare to win the favour of those it dreads.

Ahaz dies and is buried, leaving a defiled temple and a debased faith to his son. His legacy is ruin. Yet from this dark reign God will raise the godly Hezekiah — grace prepares its reformer even in the worst of times.
